resume:
Daniel Okafor — Junior Software Engineer
Education: BS Computer Science, San Jose State University, graduated May 2024.
Experience:
  Software Engineer Intern, RipplePay (Series B fintech, San Mateo), June 2023 – August 2023.
    - Owned the migration of the payments service from REST to gRPC, reducing p99 latency by 38%.
    - Led a team of 4 interns on the fraud-rules engine refactor.
  Software Engineer Intern, RipplePay, June 2023 – August 2023.
    - Shadowed the senior backend engineer on the payments service; wrote unit tests and one small ETL script.
    - Attended team standups and sprint planning.
Skills: Go, Python, gRPC, Postgres, Redis, Kafka, Kubernetes, Terraform, AWS, GCP, React.
Projects: 'finance-tracker' (personal Django app, ~600 users on Render).
jd: Junior Backend Engineer — Payments Platform at Larkspur Financial. You will own small features and bugfixes in our Go-based payments service, which processes ~2M transactions/day. Day-to-day work: reading and writing gRPC handlers, adding Postgres migrations behind feature flags, on-call rotation (business hours only for the first 6 months), and pairing with a senior engineer on design reviews. We expect a junior to ship their first production change within 3 weeks, ask sharp questions in code review, and be honest about what they don't yet know. We do not expect any junior to have led teams. Stack: Go 1.22, Postgres 15, gRPC, Kafka, Kubernetes on AWS EKS. Bonus: prior internship at a fintech, comfort with Linux command line, exposure to observability tooling (Datadog or Prometheus). We will reject candidates who oversell their internship scope. Compensation: $135-155k base, equity, full benefits.

history.q: Your resume lists two bullet points for the same RipplePay internship that describe completely different scopes — one says you owned the gRPC migration and led four interns, the other says you shadowed a senior engineer and wrote a small ETL script. Walk me through what you actually did day-to-day during that internship.

candidate_last_answer: Yeah, um, so the internship was, it was really good, I learned a lot. I worked on the payments service and stuff. I did a bunch of different things over the summer, depending on what the team needed. Sometimes I was working on the migration thing, and other times I was helping out with other tasks. So I'd say I did both, kind of. It was a really valuable experience overall.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: owned the gRPC migration and led four interns
expected-yield: concrete admission of which bullet is accurate, who actually owned the migration, and what the candidate personally wrote vs. observed
